requirements.statement: Keep these stack choices normative until an ADR replaces one with evidence from a working implementation.

Use TypeScript for application, browser, shared-contract, programmatic build-script, and test code; limit exceptions to SQL migrations, shell entrypoints, Make targets, Dockerfiles, and generated files.
Make the long-running daemon the owner of orchestration.
Keep the browser a Control API client; do not host scheduler or workflow behavior in it.
Keep domain, persistence, adapter, API, and UI boundaries independently testable even when production uses one Node process.
Use SQLite transactions and normalized durable records as the source of truth.
Do not use in-memory actors, browser state, logs, or tracker comments as alternate decision stores.
Adopt a dependency only when it solves a current requirement.
Require maintained documentation for every dependency.
Require every dependency to support the selected Node line.
Keep each dependency replaceable behind a small boundary.
Run one configured project per service instance on Linux, macOS, and Linux under WSL in version 1.
Carry `project_id` and repository identity in durable records so later multi-project hosting needs no domain-model rewrite.
Use the latest mutually compatible Active LTS Node.js release line.
Use pnpm workspaces for package management.
Compile TypeScript in strict mode.
Use a root Makefile as the stable human and CI command interface.
Commit one lockfile at the repository root.
Use Node.js as the production runtime.
Do not require Bun, Deno, or a browser runtime to operate the service.
Before replacing Node, prove subprocess management, signals, process-tree termination, SQLite behavior, and every Core test.
Keep `apps/server` limited to the process entrypoint, Fastify Control API, and static UI hosting.
Keep `apps/web` responsible for the Vite/React operator UI.
Keep `packages/domain` responsible for entities, policies, pure transitions, and domain errors.
Keep `packages/contracts` responsible for TypeBox schemas, OpenAPI definitions, and generated-client inputs.
Keep `packages/persistence` responsible for Kysely, migrations, repositories, and transaction helpers.
Keep scheduler, claims, retries, review coordination, and merge queue in `packages/orchestration`.
Keep tracker, repository-host, Git, agent, and notification integrations in `packages/adapters`.
Keep Pino configuration, log bindings, metrics, and event projection in `packages/observability`.
Keep fixtures, fake adapters, fake clocks, and temporary databases in `packages/test-support`.
Point workspace dependencies inward from adapters, persistence, API, and UI toward contracts/domain.
Keep domain free of Fastify, React, Kysely, Pino, provider SDKs, and concrete adapters.
Forbid cycles between workspace packages.
Make CI verify the package graph.
Use React for the operator UI.
Use Vite for the operator UI build and development server.
Use TanStack Router for typed routes and URL search state.
Use TanStack Query for Control API server state.
Use TanStack Table for data-heavy operator surfaces.
Use source-owned shadcn/ui components and Tailwind CSS for accessible UI components.
Use a generated client derived from the shared OpenAPI contract.
Make the URL the source of truth for shareable filters, selected tabs, pagination, and history ranges.
Let TanStack Query own fetched server state.
Limit React component state to ephemeral presentation state.
Do not copy durable orchestration records into another client-side store.
Do not use XState as global state management.
Keep Next.js and TanStack Start out of the initial stack.
Do not add a second web-server lifecycle merely for SSR, React Server Components, or framework server functions.
Require a concrete hosted-product need before adding those web frameworks.
Do not move orchestration into request handlers when adding a web framework.
Proxy Control API and event-stream requests from the development Vite server to Node.
Serve the production Vite static build from Fastify.
Expose one application port in production.
Use Fastify for the server.
Define public request/response contracts with TypeBox schemas that provide runtime validation and JSON Schema.
Generate OpenAPI from the same schemas accepted by the server.
Fail CI when the committed OpenAPI document or generated client is stale.
Use ordinary HTTP resources with SPEC error, authorization, expected-version, and idempotency contracts.
Keep tRPC and GraphQL out of the initial stack.
Do not substitute shared TypeScript types for a language-neutral wire contract.
Deliver live logs, stage transitions, usage, budgets, and other append-only updates to browsers through SSE.
Give SSE events durable cursors that support resume or explicit refresh after a gap.
Keep mutations as ordinary authenticated HTTP requests.
Keep API and orchestration module boundaries separate.
Keep API and orchestration shutdown paths separate.
Prevent an HTTP failure from corrupting scheduler state.
Prevent scheduler backpressure from making health endpoints unavailable.
Prevent scheduler backpressure from making operator-control endpoints unavailable.
Model durable orchestration with explicit TypeScript discriminated unions.
Compute orchestration transitions with pure functions.
Apply orchestration commands through transactional handlers.
Load the current durable aggregate and revision before a transition.
Validate the typed event or terminal result before a transition.
Compute decisions with a pure domain function.
Atomically persist state, events, claim changes, and side-effect intents.
Apply only committed intents through typed adapters.
Persist side-effect receipts and reconcile the observed external revision.
Do not use XState actor snapshots as durable truth.
Keep Temporal, BullMQ, Redis, and other workflow/queue services out of the initial stack.
Use SQLite as the initial durable scheduler queue.
Route time, IDs, randomness, process launch, filesystem, network, and provider calls through explicit orchestration interfaces.
Let tests replace every named external boundary with fakes without monkey-patching globals.
Use SQLite WAL mode where the deployment filesystem supports it.
Use `better-sqlite3` as the Node SQLite driver.
Use Kysely as the typed SQL query builder.
Use ordered, immutable, repository-owned migrations.
Map database rows to domain types at the persistence boundary.
Change Kysely table interfaces, migrations, and row mappers together.
Include a migration for every schema change.
Include a migration test for every schema change.
Do not edit an applied migration.
Use expand-migrate-contract for destructive changes unless release policy proves no durable installation can observe an intermediate version.
Persist state transitions in explicit transactions.
Persist claim mutations in explicit transactions.
Persist budget reservations in explicit transactions.
Persist review aggregates in explicit transactions.
Create side-effect intents in explicit transactions.
Do not build SQL with untrusted input strings.
Test persistence against real temporary SQLite databases.
Run production migrations in persistence tests.
Do not use mocked query-builder chains as the sole repository evidence.
Optimize the initial implementation honestly for SQLite.
Optimize the initial implementation honestly for a single writer.
Do not claim drop-in PostgreSQL support merely because Kysely has another dialect.
Use Pino for process-level structured logging.
Use Fastify's Pino integration for request logging instead of a separate logger.
Emit newline-delimited JSON in production.
Give each component a child logger with stable bindings.
Bind applicable service-run, project, work, attempt, session, repository, and stage identifiers.
Store event names and reason codes as fields, not parsed message text.
Use one root configuration for levels, serializers, and redaction.
Remove authorization headers, cookies, credentials, secret references, full prompts, raw diffs, and provider tokens before data leaves the process.
Do not let user-controlled values define redaction paths.
Write SPEC-required events and logs to SQLite before projecting them to Pino.
Do not derive guard, retry, budget, or merge decisions by reading process logs.
Use Vitest for unit, contract, component, and Node integration tests.
Use Playwright for browser and end-to-end tests.
Use Biome for formatting and linting.
Use TypeScript `noEmit` for type checking.
Use real temporary repositories, processes, and SQLite databases for boundary integration tests.
Unit-test pure policy and transition functions.
Run every adapter against shared conformance fixtures.
Integration-test migrations.
Integration-test transactions.
Integration-test restart recovery.
Integration-test process-tree termination.
Integration-test intent/receipt reconciliation.
Browser-test every required UI surface.
Browser-test security-sensitive mutations.
Establish an initial measured coverage floor in CI.
Prevent unexplained coverage regression below the measured floor.
Do not reward low-value tests written only to raise coverage.
Make `make verify` the canonical full local and CI gate.
Make CI compose the same repository-owned commands used locally.
Support development on Linux, macOS, and Linux under WSL.
Provide `make setup` to install pinned tools and repository hooks.
Provide `make dev` to run web and server under one supervisor.
Provide `make build` for all production artifacts.
Provide `make test` for the normal test suite.
Provide `make verify` for the complete local/CI gate.
Provide `make start` for the built production service.
Keep both development processes in one terminal.
Forward termination signals to both development process trees.
Return failure when either required development process exits unexpectedly.
Bind the server to the SPEC loopback default.
Print the exact UI URL after startup.
Do not require or attempt to launch a graphical browser.
Make a WSL-hosted service usable from Windows through localhost forwarding.
Document non-loopback binding as a security-sensitive exception, not a troubleshooting default.
Support a normal Node.js production installation.
Provide a multi-stage Docker image containing the built server, static UI, production dependencies, and required OS utilities.
Run the production image as a non-root user.
Support a read-only root filesystem where deployment permits it.
Write only to declared database, workspace, and temporary volumes.
Expose health checks that distinguish startup/recovery from readiness.
Keep build credentials and source-control tokens out of the image.
Accept secrets only at runtime.
Preserve signal delivery to the Node process.
Document container workspace mounts explicitly.
Document container Git identity explicitly.
Document repository-host credentials explicitly.
Document agent credentials explicitly.
Document the agent executable boundary explicitly.
Do not mount a host Docker socket by default.
Do not describe a host Docker socket as ordinary isolation.
Do not require Docker for contributors.
Add a self-contained executable only after subprocess, native-dependency, and SQLite Core conformance passes on every supported host.
Select the newest stable, non-prerelease dependency version on first adoption.
Require dependency compatibility with Node, peers, build tools, native modules, and supported hosts.
Interpret “latest” as latest compatible at the time of change.
Do not use unpinned floating versions resolved on each CI run.
Record exact resolved versions in `pnpm-lock.yaml`.
Pin the package manager in `package.json`.
Constrain Node.js in `engines`.
Use one repository-owned toolchain version file across developers, CI, and Docker builds.
Execute container base images immutably.
Execute GitHub Actions immutably.
Use automated pull requests to keep action and image pins current.
Require a prerelease-dependency ADR to state the missing stable capability, operational risk, rollback path, and removal condition.
Do not adopt an incompatible transitive dependency to preserve a preferred top-level package.
Do not adopt an unmaintained transitive dependency to preserve a preferred top-level package.
Make automated dependency updates follow `CICD.md`.
Validate dependency updates with the same gates as application code.
Isolate major dependency upgrades from unrelated changes.
Review release notes, migrations, peer changes, and runtime support for each major upgrade.
Do not add multi-project configuration to one running instance before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add multi-tenant authorization or tenant isolation before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add PostgreSQL before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add horizontally scaled schedulers before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add remote worker fleets before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add WebSockets before a measured bidirectional-streaming need.
Do not add server-side rendering before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add a Go, Rust, or Elixir service component before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not use XState as a backend workflow engine before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add Temporal before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add Redis before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add a general-purpose job queue before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add a self-contained desktop application before a measured revisit trigger.
Do not add self-contained executable distribution before a measured revisit trigger.
Reconsider a deferred choice only when a measured requirement appears.
Do not treat preference, novelty, or theoretical future scale as evidence for a deferred choice.
Make every deferred-choice replacement preserve the normative SPEC contracts.
Give every deferred-choice replacement a migration path for durable state.
Make every deferred-choice replacement pass the applicable conformance profile.
